differential_diagnoses.justification: This is the explicit radiological impression from the X-ray.
Adenoid tissue naturally grows during childhood, and some degree of enlargement can be considered physiological.
Acute or chronic inflammation due to infection can cause adenoid enlargement, presenting similarly to hypertrophy.

further_investigations: Polysomnography (sleep study) if sleep-disordered breathing is suspected
management_plan.follow_up_timeline: Routine outpatient review with an ENT specialist.

parsed.patient_explanation: The X-ray shows that the adenoids, which are soft tissues at the back of your child's nose, are larger than usual. This is a common finding in children and can sometimes make it harder to breathe through the nose, especially at night, and might lead to snoring or ear problems. A specialist will discuss the best way to manage this.

triage_priority.justification: The report indicates adenoidal hypertrophy but specifies no immediate recommendations or urgency, suggesting a routine outpatient review is appropriate.
